front: Novel: Constance's affair with gamekeeper Oliver Mellors
Novel: subject of a landmark 1960 R v Penguin Books Ltd obscenity trial
Novel: opens with 'Ours is essentially a tragic age, so we refuse to take it tragically'
Novel: Clifford Chatterley is paralyzed from the waist down and owns the Wragby estate; tended to by Mrs. Bolton
Novel: Mellors is estranged from wife Bertha Coutts; Constance claims her child's father is painter Duncan Forbes
Novel: Constance rejects the Irish playwright Michaelis before beginning her affair with the gamekeeper
Novel: Clifford rants about coal mine workers before his motorized wheelchair gets stuck on a hill; Mellors frees it
Novel: two characters name their genitals 'Lady Jane' and 'John Thomas'
Novel: Philip Larkin's 'Annus Mirabilis' opens and closes by mentioning this novel before 'The Beatles' first LP'
Novel: Paul Morel cannot leave his mother Gertrude for Clara Dawes or Miriam Leivers
Novel: Paul mercy-kills his cancer-ridden mother with morphine
Novel: set in a Nottinghamshire coal mining neighborhood called The Bottoms
Novel: Gertrude Morel is furious when her drunken husband cuts son William's hair while he sleeps
Novel: Clara Dawes is a suffragette; her estranged husband Baxter is a metalworker who pushes Paul's boss down stairs
Novel: editor Edward Garnett cut roughly a tenth of the manuscript to make it publishable
Novel: sequel to The Rainbow; Gudrun and Ursula Brangwen pair with sculptor Loerke's friend Gerald Crich and Rupert Birkin; climactic trip to the Alps
Novel: Gudrun Brangwen based on Katherine Mansfield
Novel: Rupert Birkin forces a terrified mare to stand by a railway crossing (David Lodge's model of symbolism)
Novel: follows three generations of the Brangwen family from patriarch Tom to Ursula; ends with Ursula seeing a rainbow after Anton Skrebensky abandons her
Novel: Ursula has a sapphic romance with schoolteacher Winifred Inger, who later marries her coal baron uncle
Poem: speaker regrets 'a pettiness' after hurling a 'clumsy log' at a creature drinking from his water-trough; calls it 'a king in exile, uncrowned in the underworld'
Poem: title flowers possess 'the smoking blueness of Pluto's gloom'; used as a torch to journey to the underworld
Poem: 'manhood is cast / Down in the flood of remembrance'; speaker's heart weeps to belong in his childish days
Short Story: Elizabeth Bates discovers husband Walter dead in a coal mine
Short Story: chrysanthemums symbolize Elizabeth Bates's changing attitudes toward her marriage
Short Story: a house whispers 'there must be more money'; Paul screams 'It's Malabar!' before dying
Short Story: Paul wins 80,000 pounds for his mother Hester; uncle Oscar Cresswell and gardener Bassett join the betting scheme
Short Story: an officer torments his orderly until the orderly kills him; title story of a collection also containing 'Odour of Chrysanthemums'
Work: 1923 critical study covering Benjamin Franklin, James Fenimore Cooper, and Herman Melville; W.H. Auden praised it as a model of great criticism
back: Studies in Classic American Literature (D.H. Lawrence)

front: Author: lived at Mabel Dodge Luhan's ranch in Taos, New Mexico; ashes interred there; 'forbidden' paintings held at the La Fonda hotel
Author: Aldous Huxley based the character Mark Rampion in Point Counter Point on this author
Novel: protagonist Richard Somers meets Ben Cooley, a former soldier plotting to overthrow Australia's government; inspired the Jindyworobak literary movement
